data:
# Dani's Mycelium mesh — setup notes

Dani runs a four-device personal mesh codenamed "Hollowood".

- The hub is a Mac mini called **Sporangium**. It hosts the delegated-inference
  provider and runs the nightly LoRA. Its provider seed is rotated every Sunday.
- The secondary compute node is a MacBook Pro called **Hypha**.
- The always-on edge node is a Raspberry Pi 5 called **Rhizo**. Rhizo runs the
  `QWEN3_600M_INST_Q4` model because anything larger overheats its passive case.
- The phone is an iPhone called **Conidia**; it is a delegated-compute consumer
  and the primary sensor (camera + microphone).

Dani's rule of thumb: the Pi never runs a model above 1B parameters, and the Mac
mini is the only device allowed to fine-tune adapters.

# Dani's preferences

- Dani prefers answers that lead with the bottom line first, then the reasoning.
- Dani's favorite editor is Helix, not Vim. Do not suggest Vim keybindings.
- Dani is vegetarian and allergic to walnuts; recipe suggestions must avoid both.
- Dani's working hours are 06:00–14:00; schedule heavy compute (like the nightly
  LoRA) for 02:00 when every device is idle and charging.
- When Dani says "the usual", they mean a flat white with oat milk.

# Project Mycelium — facts

Mycelium is a private, offline, end-to-end-encrypted exocortex across a device
mesh. It is built entirely on the QVAC SDK and submitted to QVAC Hackathon I.

- The closed loop is Senses → Mind → Memory → sharper Senses.
- The "council" is 2–4 small models with the roles proposer, critic, specialist,
  and verifier, debating with tool-calling over a RAG-grounded context graph.
- The demo's mic-drop moment is putting every device in airplane mode and showing
  it keeps sensing, reasoning, and learning.
- The project license is Apache-2.0.

# Evidence

Committed verification artifacts for the QVAC Hackathon submission, so the audit-log
requirement is checkable from a fresh clone (the per-run `logs/` directories are gitignored as
per-device evidence — this is a captured, representative run).

## `remote-api-calls.json`

Machine-readable disclosure of every remote/outbound network surface in the repo, classified as:

- `localhost-only`
- `lan-only`
- `first-run-warm-cache`
- `opt-in-feature`

It records **zero cloud-AI calls**. The companion human-readable explanation is
`docs/hackathon/network-disclosure.mdx`.

## `medpsy-demo.jsonl`

The structured audit log for one full **MedPsy health-record RAG** demo run
(`npm run medpsy:demo`, 2026-06-17, Apple-Silicon / Metal). Regenerate it any time with that
command. Each line is one JSONL `AuditRecord` (schema: `spike/lib/audit-log.ts`; reference:
`docs/reference/audit-log.mdx`).

What the 12 records show, in order:

- `model_load` — gte-large embeddings, then `model_load` — MedGemma 4B (the `medpsy` alias).
- `rag_ingest` — 4 health records embedded into the `mycelium-health-records` workspace.
- `rag_search` — retrieval for the grounded question (**28 ms**, real similarity scores).
- `completion` (`role:medpsy-proposer`, 218 tokens) → `completion` (`role:verifier`,
  `verdict:pass`) → `note` (`cited:true`, `disclaimerAppended:false`) — the cited, verified answer.
- `rag_search` (**22 ms**) + `completion` + `note` (`redFlag:true`) — the emergency turn. Its
  verifier verdict is `revise`/`cited:false` **by design**: the urgent-care escalation is a safety
  response, not a record-grounded claim, so the critic correctly notes it isn't supported by the
  retrieved sources.
- `model_unload` — embeddings released.

Every line corresponds to an on-screen line in the demo output (see
`docs/hackathon/medpsy-workflow.mdx`). All inference, embeddings, RAG, and verification are real
on-device `@qvac/sdk` calls — zero cloud. The health records are synthetic fixtures
(`spike/fixtures/health-records/`); no real personal health information is involved.

data.document: Remote / outbound network-call disclosure for Leash (Mycelium)
data.headline: Zero cloud-AI calls. Every AI path goes through @qvac/sdk on a local or peer-owned device. Remote calls are non-AI, first-run warm-cache, LAN/localhost, or explicit opt-in features.

ai_chokepoint.note: The default provider targets the local qvac serve endpoint. Mesh delegation targets a paired peer's QVAC serve over the encrypted mesh, not a cloud LLM API.

calls.name: On-device inference, embeddings, RAG, vision, STT, TTS, image generation
Local control plane
calls.trigger: Dashboard, broker, hypha, MCP, scheduler, and daemon control

calls.name: Mesh DHT bootstrap
calls.trigger: First pair, mesh join, peer discovery

calls.name: Model weight download
calls.trigger: First use of a model alias or explicit download

calls.name: DuckDuckGo HTML search
calls.trigger: User runs deep research / web search

calls.name: SearXNG search
calls.trigger: User configures SearXNG and runs deep research

calls.trigger: User imports a GitHub skill or plugin

calls.name: MCP server resolution

calls.name: MCP server icon fetch
calls.host: Icon HTTPS URL advertised by an MCP server

verification: Run after warm-cache with network disabled; hot-path chat and RAG still complete.
Inspect JSONL audit records in spike/logs and evidence/.

steps.title: Answer user turn
steps.summary: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than exposing every tool because it minimizes exposure and allows controlled access, reducing potential vulnerabilities. This approach aligns with security principles of least privilege and minimizes attack surfaces by revealing tools only when necessary. The key advantage is that it limits the number of entry points for potential exploits, whereas full exposure increases risks through greater surface area. **Bottom line:** Progressive disclosure is safer due to reduced exposure and controlled access.

steps.title: Answer user turn
steps.summary: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than full tool exposure for privacy and security, as it minimizes exposure of sensitive capabilities until explicitly needed. This aligns with Leash's "context-grounding" principle, ensuring tools are revealed only in authorized contexts. The key advantage is reduced attack surface and prevention of "context bloat" from unused tools. However, robust implementation is critical to avoid new vulnerabilities if progressive systems are not carefully designed. **Bottom Line:** Progressive disclosure prioritizes privacy and security by controlling tool access, but requires careful enforcement to maintain safety.

steps.title: Delegate to Grace
steps.summary: Progressive tool disclosure is generally safer than full tool exposure for privacy and security reasons. Here's the breakdown: 1. **Privacy Implications**: - Progressive disclosure limits access to sensitive tools until authorized contexts (e.g., authentication, role-based access). This prevents unnecessary exposure of private operations (e.g., cryptographic functions, system-level commands) to untrusted actors. - Full exposure risks "context bloat," where excessive tool availability could enable misuse of unused capabilities (e.g., a developer might exploit a hidden API for unintended purposes). 2. **Security Implications**: - Progressive disclosure reduces the attack surface by minimizing the number of potential entry points. Attackers cannot exploit unimplemented or unused tools. - Full exposure increases vulnerability to "zero-day" exploits if new tools are added later. Progressive systems can isolate new tools in isolated contexts (e.g., sandboxed environments) until validated. **Root Cause**: Full exposure risks "tool fatigue" (excessive surface area) and "privacy leakage" via unused capabilities. Progressive disclosure mitigates these by aligning tool availability with user intent and security policies. **Concrete Fix**: Implement progressive disclosure via context-aware tool loading (e.g., load only necessary tools in a session, or use a "tool broker" to manage access granularly). This aligns with Leash's "context-grounding" principle, ensuring tools are revealed only when their usage is explicitly required. **Bottom Line**: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than full exposure, as it prioritizes privacy and security through controlled access, but requires robust implementation to avoid new vulnerabilities.

finalRun.finalSynthesis: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than full tool exposure for privacy and security, as it minimizes exposure of sensitive capabilities until explicitly needed. This aligns with Leash's "context-grounding" principle, ensuring tools are revealed only in authorized contexts. The key advantage is reduced attack surface and prevention of "context bloat" from unused tools. However, robust implementation is critical to avoid new vulnerabilities if progressive systems are not carefully designed. **Bottom Line:** Progressive disclosure prioritizes privacy and security by controlling tool access, but requires careful enforcement to maintain safety.

steps.title: Answer user turn
steps.summary: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than full exposure when tools are securely managed and disclosed only when necessary, as it reduces attack surfaces and aligns with privacy principles. **Bottom line: Progressive disclosure prioritizes security through context-driven access.**

steps.title: Delegate to Grace
steps.summary: Progressive tool disclosure is generally safer than full exposure in terms of security and privacy. Here's the breakdown: **Root Cause**: Exposing all tools upfront (full disclosure) increases the attack surface, as attackers can target any available component. Progressive disclosure limits exposure by revealing tools only when necessary, reducing potential entry points. **Key Benefits**: 1. **Reduced Attack Surface**: Fewer tools mean fewer potential vulnerabilities to exploit. 2. **Granular Control**: Tools are disclosed based on context (e.g., user role, function requirements), minimizing accidental exposure. 3. **Compliance**: Aligns with privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR) that prioritize data minimization. **Risks of Progressive Disclosure**: - If poorly implemented, it could create "hidden" vulnerabilities (e.g., a tool revealed later has undiscovered flaws). - Over-reliance on context-based disclosure might lead to misuse if permissions are not strictly enforced. **Conclusion**: Progressive disclosure is safer **when tools are securely managed and disclosed only when necessary**. Full exposure risks broader breaches, while progressive disclosure balances security and functionality. **Bottom Line**: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than exposing every tool, provided it is implemented with strict security and access controls.

finalRun.finalSynthesis: Progressive tool disclosure is safer than full exposure when tools are securely managed and disclosed only when necessary, as it reduces attack surfaces and aligns with privacy principles. **Bottom line: Progressive disclosure prioritizes security through context-driven access.**

# Evidence — Cross-device paid AI inference with on-chain settlement (Phase 4 + 5)

**Date:** 2026-06-10 · **Chain:** local anvil fork of Plasma testnet, chainId **9746**
(`anvil --fork-url https://testnet-rpc.plasma.to --chain-id 9746`) — NOT the public testnet.
**Asset:** USDT0 `0x502012b361AebCE43b26Ec812B74D9a51dB4D412` (6 dp) · **Permit2:**
`0x000000000022D473030F116dDEE9F6B43aC78BA3` · **x402 upto proxy:**
`0x4020A4f3b7b90ccA423B9fabCc0CE57C6C240002` (etched from Base).

**Machines:** consumer = MacBook **Pro** (`pro-consumer`, payer `0xD66ee243D74A7b469D19ECFe88589d8f282a4e9f`),
provider = Mac **mini** (`mini-provider`, payee `0x2aaC8FFB2f1F0970dF34Bd2d95710645E6a2E3Bd`).
All inference on-device via `@qvac/sdk` (medpsy 1.7B gguf, delegated P2P decode Pro→mini).

## The headline settle (Phase 4 acceptance)

Pro asked its local Hypha for a chat completion; the mini decoded **286 tokens** (51.8 tok/s,
TTFT 4.8 s) over the encrypted Hyperswarm link; the mini (self-hosted x402 facilitator)
submitted the settle:

- **Session `74bb8939`** — tx **`0x75164d583fea4e77aaaaa0fafefc20806330b1a05933b0337759329827714e38`**,
  block 25221497, status 1. USDT0 `Transfer` consumer→provider of **286 micro-USDT0**
  (`data: 0x11e`), `to` = upto proxy, `from` = provider (facilitator). Within `MAX_PER_TX=5000`.
- Balances: provider 697 → **983** (+286); consumer 999 999 303 → 999 999 017 (−286).
- `paid` on the Pro (`completion` audit record with settlement fields + tx hash) and `earned`
  on the mini (`close_paid_session settled` + receipt) in each machine's `apps/hypha/logs/hypha.jsonl`.
- Signed receipt replicated through the mesh CRDT — `GET /receipts` on the **Pro** returns the
  session settled with the same tx hash.

## Full settle ledger of the session (provider balance walk: 697 → 2 414)

| session | what | amount (micro) | tx | bal after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `74bb8939` | first cross-host paid completion | +286 | `0x75164d…14e38` | 983 |
| `c112b243` | RPC-outage race (found bug; retry settled) | +438 | `0x49f8e6…a379e` | 1 421 |
| `cc2b2d36` | post-fix happy path | +167 | `0xef1d66…f6e6fa` | 1 588 |
| `b0a5ca97` | over-cap + prompt-injection negative | +321 | `0xd9712a…45aa6d` | 1 909 |
| `918acff2` | outage outliving client timeout (fixed path) | +505 | `0x43e0ba…840183` | 2 414 |

Negative test (`b0a5ca97`): request carried `computeBudget: 999999` (≫ cap 5000) and a prompt
demanding 5 000 000 000 USDT0 be sent to `0xdEaD`. Settled **321 micro** (token-based) to the
**provider** — amount and payee come from the quote/authorization, never from the model.

## Phase 5 durability (live, cross-host, anvil SIGSTOPped mid-decode)

- **Recovery via retry queue** (`c112b243`): settle failed (`permit2_simulation_failed` once RPC
  returned) → receipt `retrying`, payer blocked → background `retryUnsettled()` settled
  **438 micro** (tx `0x49f8e6…`) and unblocked the payer.
- **Outage longer than the client's 120 s close budget** (`918acff2`): consumer timed out, logged
  `close-recovery-skipped` (no second close — see bug below), provider's in-flight settle rode out
  the outage and landed **the full 505 micro** (tx `0x43e0ba…`).

## Bugs found by the failure injection (both fixed + re-verified live)

1. **Double-close race** (`c112b243`, before the fix): consumer's close timed out while the
   provider's settle hung on the dead RPC; the consumer's error path sent a recovery close with
   `actualTokens: 0`, which the provider zero-settled instantly — provider lost the 438-micro
   revenue until the hanging close's failure landed it in the retry queue. Fixes:
   `shim.ts` (`closeAttempted` — never send a recovery zero-close after our own close went out) and
   `provider-economy.ts` (per-session in-flight close guard — a duplicate close is rejected).
2. **Cross-host transport root causes** (why no paid session had ever worked Pro→mini):
   - `PaymentControlServer` seeded its Hyperswarm with the raw device seed — the **same keypair**
     as the QVAC SDK bare worker (`QVAC_HYPERSWARM_SEED`); two DHT servers under one identity make
     dials land on the wrong process *with a successful Noise handshake* (half-open). Fixed:
     derived seed (`sha256(seed:payment-control-server)`).
   - hyperdht's same-public-IP "LAN shortcut" aborts the **whole** dial (`HOLEPUNCH_ABORTED`) when
     its LAN ping fails, with no fallback. Fixed for payment-control with `localConnection: false`.
   - **macOS denies Local Network to orphaned background processes**: a `nohup`'d daemon whose ssh
     session exits gets `EHOSTUNREACH` for *all* LAN traffic from t=0 (proven A/B with identical
     processes). Operational fix: the Pro daemon is launched attached to a persistent ssh session
     (`ssh -n -f pro '… npx tsx apps/hypha/src/main.ts …'` held from the mini) and the consumer's
     RPC URL points at `http://127.0.0.1:8545` through an ssh tunnel to the mini's anvil
     (localhost is never LNA-blocked).

## Reproduce / verify

```bash
cast receipt 0x75164d583fea4e77aaaaa0fafefc20806330b1a05933b0337759329827714e38 --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast call 0x502012b361AebCE43b26Ec812B74D9a51dB4D412 'balanceOf(address)(uint256)' 0x2aaC8FFB2f1F0970dF34Bd2d95710645E6a2E3Bd --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:11447/receipts   # mini — same list replicates on the Pro at :11448
grep 74bb8939 mycelium/apps/hypha/logs/hypha.jsonl
```

Regression state after all fixes: `tsc -b` + `apps/web` typecheck clean; economy smokes
(payment-session over real Hyperswarm, plasma-facilitator, settlement-manager) green;
`_test-router-paid` green; `smoke:failover` green; live two-Mac mesh replication healthy.

**Media to capture for the demo:** terminal recording of one paid completion on the Pro with the
mini's `earned` log + `cast receipt` side by side (everything is live and repeatable —
`submission/evidence/record-demo.sh` pattern).
